mission.title: Install and Configure k0rdent (Kubernetes Cluster Lifecycle Manager)
mission.description: k0rdent is an open-source platform for deploying and managing Kubernetes clusters at scale. It functions as a super control plane, orchestrating multiple Kubernetes clusters across on-premises, cloud, and hybrid environments using a declarative GitOps approach. Built on Cluster API (CAPI), it supports infrastructure providers like AWS, Azure, vSphere, and bare metal. k0rdent provides centralized lifecycle management — provisioning, configuration, updates, and observability — from a single management cluster.

steps.title: Ensure you have a management cluster
steps.description:
k0rdent requires a Kubernetes cluster to serve as the management cluster. You can use any existing cluster (kind, k0s, EKS, etc.).

```bash

kubectl cluster-info

```

> Verify your kubeconfig points to the cluster that will become the k0rdent management cluster.

steps.title: Install k0rdent via Helm
steps.description:
Install the k0rdent Cluster Manager (kcm) using the official OCI Helm chart from GHCR.

```bash

helm install kcm oci://ghcr.io/k0rdent/kcm/charts/kcm --version 1.8.0 -n kcm-system --create-namespace

```

> This deploys the KCM operator which then provisions CAPI and other subcomponents. The entire process takes a few minutes.

steps.title: Wait for k0rdent components to be ready
steps.description:
The KCM operator deploys several subcomponents including Cluster API controllers. Wait for all pods to become ready.

```bash

kubectl -n kcm-system wait --for=condition=Ready pods --all --timeout=300s

```

> All pods in kcm-system should reach Running/Ready state within a few minutes.

steps.title: Verify the k0rdent installation
steps.description:
Check that the Management object and core components are healthy.

```bash

kubectl get management.k0rdent

kubectl get pods -n kcm-system

kubectl get clustertemplate -n kcm-system

```

> You should see the kcm Management resource, running controller pods, and available cluster templates.

steps.title: Configure infrastructure credentials (example: AWS)
steps.description:
To provision child clusters, k0rdent needs credentials for your infrastructure provider. This example shows AWS — adapt for Azure, vSphere, or other providers.

```bash

kubectl create secret generic aws-credentials -n kcm-system --from-literal=AccessKeyID=$AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID --from-literal=SecretAccessKey=$AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY

```

> Replace with your actual credentials. See [k0rdent docs](https://docs.k0rdent.io/latest/admin/installation/prepare-mgmt-cluster/) for provider-specific setup including Azure, vSphere, and bare metal.
commands: kubectl create secret generic aws-credentials -n kcm-system --from-literal=AccessKeyID=$AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID --from-literal=SecretAccessKey=$AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY

steps.title: Create a child cluster (example)
steps.description:
Deploy a managed child cluster using a k0rdent ClusterDeployment. This example creates a basic cluster — customize the template and parameters for your environment.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: k0rdent.mirantis.com/v1alpha1
kind: ClusterDeployment
metadata:
  name: my-child-cluster
  namespace: kcm-system
spec:
  template: aws-standalone-cp-0-1-8
  credential: aws-credentials
  config:
    region: us-east-1
    controlPlane:
      instanceType: t3.medium
    worker:
      instanceType: t3.medium
      replicas: 2
EOF

```

> This provisions a 2-worker Kubernetes cluster on AWS. Monitor progress with `kubectl get clusterdeployment -n kcm-system -w`.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: k0rdent.mirantis.com/v1alpha1
kind: ClusterDeployment
metadata:
  name: my-child-cluster
  namespace: kcm-system
spec:
  template: aws-standalone-cp-0-1-8
  credential: aws-credentials
  config:
    region: us-east-1
    controlPlane:
      instanceType: t3.medium
    worker:
      instanceType: t3.medium
      replicas: 2
EOF

uninstall.title: Uninstall k0rdent
uninstall.description:
Remove k0rdent and all managed resources from the management cluster.

```bash

kubectl delete clusterdeployments --all -n kcm-system

kubectl delete management.k0rdent kcm

helm uninstall kcm -n kcm-system

kubectl delete ns kcm-system

```

> Warning: Delete all ClusterDeployments first to properly deprovision child clusters before removing k0rdent.

upgrade.title: Upgrade k0rdent
upgrade.description:
Upgrade to a newer version of k0rdent using Helm.

```bash

helm upgrade kcm oci://ghcr.io/k0rdent/kcm/charts/kcm --version 1.8.0 -n kcm-system

kubectl -n kcm-system wait --for=condition=Ready pods --all --timeout=300s

```

> Replace the version number with the desired release. Check [k0rdent releases](https://github.com/k0rdent/kcm/releases) for available versions.

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in Pending or CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Insufficient resources on the management cluster or webhook certificate issues.

**Fix:**
Check pod events: `kubectl describe pods -n kcm-system`. Ensure your management cluster has at least 4 CPU and 8GB RAM available. For certificate issues, delete the webhook and let it regenerate.

troubleshooting.title: ClusterDeployment stuck in Provisioning
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Infrastructure credentials may be invalid or the provider may have quota limits.

**Fix:**
Check the CAPI cluster status: `kubectl get clusters -n kcm-system`. Inspect machine objects: `kubectl get machines -n kcm-system`. Verify credentials in the referenced Secret are correct and the account has sufficient permissions.

troubleshooting.title: No ClusterTemplates available
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** k0rdent may still be initializing or the Management object hasn't been fully reconciled.

**Fix:**
Wait a few minutes for the KCM operator to finish bootstrapping. Check: `kubectl get management.k0rdent kcm -o yaml` and look at the status conditions.
